coredata.dc:title: Development of a novel humidity sensor for breath pattern recognition using large dislocation hollow core fiber infused with nano-carbon quantum water gel microfiber
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yan-nan
Qin, Liyun
Yuan, Zhenyu
Li, Jin
Meng, Fanli
coredata.dc:description: This study presents a humidity sensor based on a large-core-offset hollow core optical fiber (LD-HCF) combined with polyvinyl alcohol carbon quantum dots (PVA-CQDs) nanocomposite microfiber for monitoring human respiratory. PVA-CQDs was introduced into the hollow core structure of LD-HCF to develope the respiratory sensor. The sensor has been integrated into a respiratory monitoring mask, exposed to ultraviolet (UV) light and demonstrated with a humidity sensitivity of 0.1721 nm/%RH as well as response and recovery times of 0.232 s and 0.252 s, respectively. The mask provides the real-time monitoring of human respiratory rates during various activities, including walking, standing, coughing, running, apnea 1 time and apnea 3 times. The typical respiratory patterns have been classified with a remarkable accuracy of 97.62 % using a WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural network. The respiratory monitoring device is proposed by optical fiber, offering a promising potential in the real-time monitoring for healthcare process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical fibers
Nanocomposite microfiber
Humidity sensitivity
Respiratory sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of LD-HCF humidity sensor based on PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber
Optical path transmission in LD-HCF structure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis process of PVA-CQDs hydrogel material. (b) Preparation of LD-HCF. Filling process of PVA-CQDs hydrogel after (c) 0 minutes, (d) 10 minutes, (e) 20 minutes, (f) 30 minutes. (g) PVA-CQDs is fully filled and dried in HCF.

(a) LD-HCF humidity optical fiber sensor with PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber, (b) UV light source and (c) respiratory monitoring mask. Schematic diagram of (d) working principle of LD-HCF optical fiber humidity sensor based on PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber and (e) schematic diagram of sensitization mechanism of PVA-CQDs under UV light excitation. (f) Humidity sensing system and (g) respiratory sensing experimental system.
(a) TEM image and (b) HRTEM image of CQDs. (c) Fluorescence profiles of CQDs at different concentrations and (d) FTIR spectra. XPS spectra of CQDs: (e) XPS of O and (f) XPS of C.

Comparison of different sensors, including (a) spectrogram and (b) spatial spectrogram. Comparison of humidity sensing characteristics of different sensors, including (c) humidity spectra and (d) sensitivity curves of LD-HCF structure, (e) humidity spectra and (f) sensitivity curves of LD-HCF structure filled with PVA-CQDs hydrogel, (g) humidity spectra and (h) sensitivity curves of LD-HCF structure filled with PVA-CQDs hydrogel under UV. Comparison of (i) humidity sensitivity and (j) stability of LD-HCF humidity sensor filled with PVA-CQDs with other sensors under UV light. Humidity sensing performance of respiratory mask, including (k) humidity dynamic response and (l) humidity sensitivity curve.

LD-HCF humidity sensor filled with PVA-CQDs under UV light (a) spectrum curve with temperature change and (b) temperature sensitivity curve. Humidity spectral response curves of (c) sensor-2 and (d) sensor-3, and comparison of (e) humidity sensitivity curves of different sensors.

(a) Time response curve of respiratory monitoring mask. Response curve of respiratory monitoring mask for human (b) walking, (c) resting, (d) coughing, (e) running, (f) apnea 1 time and (g) apnea 3 times. (h) Response output curve and (i) frequency response curve of breathing mask in dynamic monitoring for human breathing.
Architecture of respiratory data classification model based on WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural network.

Training results of (a, c) training set and (b, d) test set of 1D CNN-LSTM and WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural networks. (c) Comparison of loss function diagrams between 1D CNN-LSTM neural network and WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural network. Confusion matrices of different neural networks, including (f) 1D CNN-LSTM neural network and (g) WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural network.
Comparison results between this work and other reports.

head.ce:title: Development of a novel humidity sensor for breath pattern recognition using large dislocation hollow core fiber infused with nano-carbon quantum water gel microfiber

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents a humidity sensor based on a large-core-offset hollow core optical fiber (LD-HCF) combined with polyvinyl alcohol carbon quantum dots (PVA-CQDs) nanocomposite microfiber for monitoring human respiratory. PVA-CQDs was introduced into the hollow core structure of LD-HCF to develope the respiratory sensor. The sensor has been integrated into a respiratory monitoring mask, exposed to ultraviolet (UV) light and demonstrated with a humidity sensitivity of 0.1721 nm/%RH as well as response and recovery times of 0.232 s and 0.252 s, respectively. The mask provides the real-time monitoring of human respiratory rates during various activities, including walking, standing, coughing, running, apnea 1 time and apnea 3 times. The typical respiratory patterns have been classified with a remarkable accuracy of 97.62 % using a WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural network. The respiratory monitoring device is proposed by optical fiber, offering a promising potential in the real-time monitoring for healthcare process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Respiration is one of the fundamental functions of the human body. It not only maintains the supply of oxygen and removal of carbon dioxide, but also reflects an individual's physiological and psychological state. Despite, respiration can act crucial health indicator to diagnosis various diseases, including sleep apnea, asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and anemia [1]. For examples, Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) is the most common pediatric neuromuscular disease. The patients often experience sleep-related breathing disorders. Timely assessment of the quality of sleep-related breathing is useful for the early-diagnosing of DMD [2]. Respiratory distress (DR) is a respiratory system disorder characterized by irregular breathing patterns. Due to its similar symptoms (shortness of breath, tachycardia, and dizziness) to other common cardiopulmonary diseases such as chronic obstructive pulmonary and asthma, DR is often underdiagnosed or misdiagnosed. The high misdiagnosis rate of DR indicates that the healthcare professionals may not fully understand the condition, making it difficult to provide the appropriate treatment[3]. Furthermore, sleep-disordered breathing (SDB) caused by the repeated occurrence of partial or complete collapse of the upper respiratory tract or respiratory tract narrowing can lead to the respiratory pauses and insufficiency, along with intermittent hypoxemia, hypercapnia, and the activation of sympathetic nervous system. This is closely related to heart failure (HF) and poses a threat to human life [4]. In the context of the global COVID-19 pandemic, it has never been more critical for the continuous monitoring of human respiratory status. By measuring respiratory rate [5], intensity [6], duration [7], and unique acoustic features [8], various respiratory system diseases based on coughing can be diagnosed to facilitate the timely diagnosis of COVID-19 [9].

Various solutions for humidity sensing have been widely proposed, including flexible sensors [10,11], skin sweat sensors [12,13], strain sensors [14], and optical fiber sensors [15]. Pencil-on-paper humidity sensors for health monitoring and skin characterization are capable of humidity detection in the 5.6 %RH-90 %RH range [16]. The skin interface super hydrophobic insensitive sweat sensor can detect the moisture on the skin surface and has ultra-high sensitivity [17]. The deformation sensor prepared using a 3D curved surface can achieve synchronous detection of humidity and respiration, offering the advantage of being wearable [18]. As an independent sensing unit, the self-powered stretching platform can achieve low power consumption and portability [19]. However, humidity sensors based on flexible substrates often incur higher costs, and the contact sensing method can contaminate the probe. Optical fiber humidity sensors have the advantages of small size, easy integration, and non-contact measurement. At the same time, as an independent sensing unit, optical fiber sensors can be cascaded with other optical fiber sensing units (such as gratings and special fibers) to enable multi-mode detection of various signals [20,21]. This has attracted much attention in the field of humidity sensing [22,23]. The optical fiber humidity sensor coated with carbon-nanotube/polyvinyl alcohol has high sensitivity to the humidity of 67 %RH-90 %RH, but low sensitivity to less than 67 %RH, which limits the detection range of humidity [24]. Graphene oxide-deposited tilted fiber grating for ultrafast humidity has afast response time, but the humidity sensitivity of 18.5 pm/%RH is relatively small [25]. The sensitivity of SnO2/PVA nanofiber-coated inclined fiber grating is related to the thickness of the sensitive film, which needs to be accurately controlled by the coating process during the preparation of the humidity sensor, making it relatively complicated [26]. The ETFBG humidity sensor based on CV/Nafion composite film can be widely used in the humidity detection of gases, liquids, and solids, but its long response time (4.4 s) and recovery time (4.6 s) may not be suitable for respiratory sensing [27]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a humidity sensor that can achieve non-contact detection, low cost, and high linearity.

In this study, nano-carbon quantum water gel microfiber was prepared by injecting polyvinyl alcohol and fluorescent carbon quantum dots gel composites (PVA-CQDs) into HCF for the first time. The experimental results show that the new optical fiber humidity sensor has fast response and recovery times and demonstrates good linearity across a wide humidity range. Its high sensitivity and rapid response allow real-time monitoring of humidity changes in breath, providing detailed breathing pattern data. The WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural network can extract and classify the sensor data, improving the reliability and accuracy of breathing pattern classification results. The respiratory monitoring scheme using an optical fiber humidity sensor and neural network pattern recognition mentioned in this study aids in the diagnosis and treatment of respiratory diseases. It can achieve health monitoring and early disease warning, offering potential application value in the field of intelligent medical devices.

Citric acid (ACS, 99.8 %) and p-phthalic acid (ACS, 99 %) obtained in Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology. Co. PVA (ACS, 99 %), purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. All reagents used in this work without further purification. Ultra-pure water was used in the whole experiment.

The specific synthesis process of PVA-CQDs hydrogel is illustrated in Fig. 1(a). Involved the dissolving of 13.04 wt% citric acid in 20 ml of p-phthalic acid. The mixture was then subjected to continuous reaction conditions and underwent a hydrothermal reaction at 160 °C for 6 h. Upon completion of the reaction process, the product was separated through centrifugation and subjected to continuously dialysis purification for 72 h to remove any unreacted molecular precursors, resulting in a solution of carboxylated carbon quantum dots (CQDs-COOH, hereafter referred to as CQDs). PVA as a common humidity-sensitive material, exhibits increased optical transmission loss at high concentrations and decreased sensor sensitivity at low concentrations. Based on previous studies [28,29], a concentration of 5 wt% was selected for the PVA solution in this study. Specifically, 0.5 g of PVA particles were dissolved in 9.5 ml of deionized water and stirred in a water bath at 80 °C for 2 h to accelerate dissolution, resulting in a 5 wt% PVA solution. The resulting CQDs solution was freeze-dried for 4 h to obtain a solid powder. The CQDs powder was then dissolved in deionized water in the following proportions: 0.1 g in 9.9 ml, 0.4 g in 9.6 ml, 0.8 g in 9.2 ml, 1.2 g in 8.8 ml, and 1.6 g in 8.4 ml. This produced CQDs solutions with concentrations of 0.1 wt%, 0.4 wt%, 0.8 wt%, 1.2 wt%, and 1.6 wt%, respectively, for reserve. Different mass percentages of CQDs solution were mixed with a 5 wt% PVA solution under the ultrasonic agitation for 24 h to prevent the solidifying, ultimately forming the aqueous solution of PVA-CQDs hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of LD-HCF humidity sensor based on PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication process of the LD-HCF humidity sensor based on CQDs hydrogel microfiber is show in the Fig. 1(b). The SMF is fused with HCF with an inner diameter of 19 μm and an outer diameter of 146 μm, ensuring a precise core-offset of 40 μm. During this process, it is essential to appropriately adjust the discharge intensity and discharge time to ensure the integrity of the HCF and avoid any form of collapse. A segment of the HCF is accurately cut in the length of 800 μm. On the other side of the HCF, a s SMF is also offset-fused, maintaining a 40 μm core-offset. The LD-HCF structure retains the complete structure of the HCF, preventing the collapse and sealing of the HCF air core. When liquid enters the HCF, a strong capillary effect ensures that the liquid completely fills the HCF. The PVA-CQDs solution is introduced into the HCF cross section using a syringe, and the solution self-assembles inside the HCF via capillary action, thereby completing the sensor preparation. Fig. 1(c)-(f) illustrates the infiltration length of the PVA-CQDs solution within the HCF at different time points (0 minutes, 5 minutes, 10 minutes, 15 minutes) to emphasize the filling process. The sensor at this time is placed in an incubator at 25 °C to dry for 2 h, so that the water inside the hydrogel slowly evaporates to avoid rapid shrinkage of the gel material, resulting in uneven distribution of the gel along the fiber axis. In addition, using the capillary action of HCF to fill the PVA-CQDs can make the hydrogel more uniform in the fiber, so as to ensure the axial uniformity of the hydrogel. Fig. 1(g) shows the HCF diagram after gel filling. It can be seen that hydrogel materials are evenly distributed in HCF and have axial uniformity. The sensor is prepared using HCF as a mold, with the PVA-CQDs solution introduced via capillary action. The cured PVA-CQDs gel is similar to the fiber filled within the HCF and is more uniform axially due to the presence of the HCF. Compared to the coating method, the HCF binding effect makes the PVA-CQDs easier to form, rendering the preparation process more controllable.

A breathing mask was prepared by fixing an optical fiber humidity sensor in a common breathing mask. The specific method is as follows: The optical fiber welding points are encapsulated in a heat shrink tube, and the humidity sensing area is kept exposed and in contact with air. The sensor is then embedded into the inner airflow channel of a common medical mask by means of a clamp and adhesive to ensure that the sensor has direct access to the moisture exhaled by the wearer. The cable extends of optical fiber sensor to the outside of the mask and is connected to an external monitoring device for real-time monitoring of changes in humidity during breathing. In addition, the UV lamp (λ=375 nm, 10 W, Shenzhen, Yingfeng Optoelectronics) is fixed inside the breathing mask through the adhesive, and the vertical distance from the probe is maintained at 10 cm, as shown in Fig. 2(a). The prepared breathing mask is shown in Fig. 2(b), and the mask is convenient to wear, as shown in Fig. 2(c).

The respiratory experiment was carried out by volunteer wearing a breathing mask. The temperature of the experiment was kept at 25 °C, and the breathing signals generated by the breathing mask were recorded during different physical activities of the volunteer, including walking, restiing, coughing, running, apnea 1 time and apnea 3 times. These modes of activity can generate respiratory signals of different frequencies, and are used in the study of respiratory sensor classification of respiratory patterns. Through the analysis of these signals, the ability of the sensor to recognize and distinguish various breathing patterns can be studied, and data support can be provided to further optimize the performance and application of the sensor.

The sample was characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). XPS (Thermo Scientific, America) analyzed the chemical composition and elemental states of the sample using an Al Kα radiation source, determining the oxidation states of different elements. FTIR (Thermo Fisher IS5, USA) investigated the chemical bond composition of the sample within the spectral range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1, revealing the functional group structures in the material. TEM (Tecnai G2 F20, America) was used to analyze the morphology and microstructure of the sample. Additionally, steady-state/transient fluorescence spectroscopy (Edinburgh FLS1000) was employed to analyze the fluorescence spectrum of the sample in the spectral range of 300 nm to 800 nm, exploring its optical properties.

The characteristic of PVA is that its molecular structure contains hydroxyl functional groups. These functional groups promote the formation of hydrogen bonds (HBs) among PVA molecules. The formation of HBs leads to the folding, entanglement, and cross-linking of PVA molecular chains while reducing the density of PVA molecules [30]. However, the PVA-CQDs hydrogel is even more remarkable. The improving mechanism of humidity sensitivity is shown in the Fig. 2(e). As shown in the first step of Fig. 2(e), the carboxyl functional groups on the surface of CQDs possess a certain degree of polarity, allowing them to form HBs with the soluble hydroxyl functional groups in PVA molecules. This interaction causes cross-linking between CQDs and PVA, resulting in the formation of a CQDs-PVA molecular chain structure within the PVA material. In the second step of Fig. 2(e), under the condition of UV light exposure, CQDs emits a fluorescence signal to excite electrons on the surface-active functional group and initiate the charge transfer process. This charge transfer not only affects the charge distribution of the functional groups, but also alters the nature and strength of the interactions between functional groups and surrounding molecules. Specifically, the primary effect of UV light exposure is observed within the hydrogen bond (HB) network inside the nanocarbon quantum dot hydrogel. This includes the formation, breaking, or rearrangement of HBs. The fluorescence signal emitted by CQDs leads to the break and recombination of HBs between CQDs and PVA molecular chains, forming a single PVA molecule, which increases the density of PVA molecules. Hydroxyl functional groups in PVA molecules can form HBs with surrounding water molecules. Therefore, the rearrangement of HB network may lead to stronger HBs between PVA molecules and water molecules as shown in the third step of Fig. 2(e), which further enhances the hydrophilicity of PVA. At the same time, the absorption of water molecules will lead to the shift of the emission peak position and the potential decrease of emission intensity of PVA-CQDs, which will affect the photoluminescence properties of PVA-CQDs and change the refractive index of PVA-CQDs materials [31]. This series of interacting mechanisms results in the excellent hygroscopic performance of PVA-CQDs hydrogel.

The principle of humidity measurement under UV light excitation can be described more clearly as follows: CQDs emit fluorescence under the excitation of UV light, which promotes the rearrangement of HBs in hydrogels of CQDs. This effect leads to the increase of PVA molecular density in the hydrogel, and more PVA molecules combine with water molecules through HBs, which leads to the expansion of PVA hydrogel and the change of refractive index [32–34]. This change causes the internal sensing optical path of the sensor to be adjusted as shown in Fig. 2(d). The transmission light of the HCF cladding mode also changes as the PVA-CQDs absorbs moisture. The light in PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber and cladding light form interference light of Eq. (1).
(1)

From this, the formula for calculating the wavelength of the interference light can be derived as Eq. (3):

As the external humidity changes, the refractive index and volume of the PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber change. Therefore, the humidity sensitivity of the sensor is as shown in Eq. (4):
(4)
d
λ

d H u m i d i t y

P V A − C Q D s

P V A − C Q D s

2 m + 1
Where γ
PVA-CQDs is the coefficient for the refractive index change of the PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber with respect to humidity, and α
PVA-CQDs is the coefficient for the volume change of the PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber with respect to humidity. Interference light carries sensitive information about external humidity, allowing for the measurement and monitoring of changes in environmental humidity.

TEM is employed to detailed analyze the morphology of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the CQDs exhibit the uniform and mono dispersion, displaying a spherical morphology at the microscale.The diameter distribution of CQDs falls within the range of 3–5 nm in Fig. 3(b), indicating a relatively narrow size distribution.

According to previous studies, CQDs solutions with too low concentrations exhibit low fluorescence intensity, while high concentrations lead to CQDs agglomeration and quenching [35]. Therefore, it is necessary to explore the optimal proportion of CQDs solution to achieve the best luminous effect. Fig. 3(c) displays the fluorescence intensity distribution of CQDs at different concentrations. The fluorescence emission of CQDs occurs at a wavelength around 435 nm. The highest fluorescence intensity is achieved when the CQDs' concentration is 0.8 wt%. Therefore, a 0.8 wt% mass fraction of CQDs was selected to prepare the PVA-CQDs hydrogel.

As shown in Fig. 3(d), FTIR analysis was performed on CQDs. The result clearly demonstrates the absorption characteristic peaks of -COOH and -OH functional groups. Specifically, the absorption peak at 3257 cm⁻¹ is associated with the stretching frequencies of -OH in CQDs and -OH in carboxyl groups. The peak at 1722 cm⁻¹ distinctly indicates the presence of CO functional groups in the material, further confirming the carboxylation of CQDs. Additionally, the absorption peak at 1524 cm⁻¹ primarily reflects the vibration characteristics of CC.

The XPS is used to provide the in-depth analysis of the functional groups on the surface of CQDs. The distribution of oxygen peaks in Fig. 3(e) is clearly visible, reflecting the existence of various oxidizing functional groups on the surface of CQDs. These oxygen-containing functional groups include carboxyl (COOH) and other oxygen-containing forms. Specifically, the COOH peak is located at 533.1 eV, the C-OH/C-O-C peak at 532.5 eV, and the CO peak at 531.3 eV. These XPS peaks correspond to different functional groups, providing strong evidence for their surface chemical states. Fig. 3(f) demonstrates the carbon element's peak distribution. In this case, the CC peak is situated at 248.8 eV, the C-O peak at 286.6 eV, the CO peak at 287.7 eV, and the COOH peak at 290.1 eV. These carbon element peaks further confirm the presence of carboxyl functional groups and other carbon-oxygen compounds on the surface of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical path transmission in LD-HCF structure
ce:section.ce:para: LD-HCF structure is sensitive to the refractive index change of the medium in HCF. This study conducted theoretical simulations to explore the mechanism of light propagation in the LD-HCF structure, with a particular focus on the influence of refractive indices on light propagation. Fig. S1(a) shows that when the refractive index of HCF is equal to the refractive index of air, most of the light propagates in the cladding of HCF, and only a small part of the light propagates in the air. In Fig. S1(b), the refractive index of HCF is equal to the refractive index mismatch of PVA-CQDs microfiber and the reduced refractive index mismatch leads to more light reflecting back into PVA-CQDs microfiber, which further increases the light transmission. Fig. S1(c) simulates the light propagation after- PVA-CQDs benig excited by UV light. The fluorescence of PVA-CQDs usually involves the photon absorption and re-emission, and the emitted photons usually have higher energy and frequency. This leads to an increase in the refractive index of the PVA-CQDs microfiber. The simulation results show that the penetration depth of light within the microfiber increases, and the mode field within HCF enlarges. As a result, the energy is enhanced for the transmitted light.

The humidity detection system as shown in the Fig. 2(f) is established to further study the humidity sensing characteristics of LD-HCF humidity sensor based on PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber. Humidity sensing system was presented in SI-1 in the supplementary information. In the humidity detection system, humidity is generated by the mixture of dry air and wet air. Controlling the gas flow rate can keep the humidity constant in the gas chamber. The method of humidity control by mixing dry and wet air in this study is stable and has been reported to be used in a number of studies [36–38] to maintain the flow of dry and wet air in the air chamber, and the humidity in the air chamber can remain stable for a long time to meet the experimental requirements. The specific steps are as follows: After opening the cylinder valve, adjust MFM2 to inject dry air (20 % O2/80 % N2) with a certain flow rate into the air chamber, and then control MFM1 to inject wet air with different flow rates into the air chamber to generate the target humidity environment. The humidity needs to wait for a certain time during the generation process. In addition, in order to avoid the disturbance of gas flow to the optical fiber sensor, the gas flow rate is set to less than or equal to 1.5 SLM. Since the temperature of the air exhaled by the human body is about 30 °C, the ambient temperature of the sensor humidity experiment is set at 30 °C, and the heat preservation is carried out through the air chamber. In the humidity experiment, the commercial humidity instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific-1166118, America) was used to monitor and calibrate the humidity to ensure the stability and accuracy of the humidity level in the gas chamber.

The black curve in Fig. 4(a) shows the spectrum of LD-HCF structure, which is formed by the interference of light beams in cladding and hollow core. The blue curve of Fig. 4(a) shows the spectrum of the sensor with the HCF filled by the PVA-CQDs hydrogel. The LD-HCF spectrum experiences a certain loss, mainly due to core mismatch caused by the dislocation structure and light scattering at the dislocation [39,40]. Despite this, the sensor still exhibits an interference spectrum, indicating the successful preparation of the LD-HCF structure. The light is effectively focused in the hydrogel, resulting in a decrease in the number of interference modes. This change is clearly visible in the spatial spectrum of Fig. 4(b). Fluorescence excitation experiment reveals that the fluorescence photons cause the change of refractive index of hydrogel material, which leads to the blue shift of spectrum in the red curve of Fig. 4(a).

Study the humidity sensing characteristics of the LD-HCF structure-based humidity sensor by precisely controlling the ventilation time. As shown in Fig. 4(c), the spectrum experiences a blue shift with increasing humidity, which may be due to the formation of a water film on the inner wall of HCF. Although the prepared humidity sensor has a relatively large loss, the experimental results indicate that humidity causes a change in the spectral phase rather than a change in power. Therefore, the sensor can still effectively measure humidity. The humidity sensitivity curve was fitted by tracking the three valleys in the spectrum of Fig. 4(d). The sensitivity values for the three valleys are 0.0022 nm/%RH, 0.0022 nm/%RH, and 0.0017 nm/%RH. As shown in Fig. 4(e), the spectrum has experienced the greater drift after filling PVA-CQDs. It can be seen that the sensitivity significantly increases in Fig. 4(f). This is because the hydroxyl groups in PVA-CQDs form the HBs with water molecules, and swell the PVA-CQDs microfiber [41]. The changed propagation path of light in PVA-CQDs microfiber leads to a significant blue shift in the interference spectrum. The sensitivities for the three valleys are 0.0791 nm/%RH, 0.075 nm/%RH, and 0.0908 nm/%RH, which is improved by approximately ∼41-folds higher than the former case. The humidity-sensitive spectral curve of the PVA-CQDs filled sensor under UV irradiation is shown in Fig. 4(g). The spectrum exhibits a more pronounced blue shift, and the sensitivities corresponded to different valleys are 0.1702 nm/%RH, 0.1698 nm/%RH, and 0.1721 nm/%RH, respectively (Fig. 4(h)). These values are approximately ∼2.3-folds higher than the results without UV irradiation. The enhancement of sensitivity is due to the process that the fluorescence was emitted from CQDs and further excited by UV light, which activates the HB rearrangement mechanism in the material. This mechanism leads to the breakage of HBs between PVA molecules and the increase of molecular density, and the binding ability between PVA-CQDs hydrogel and water molecules is enhanced.

Fig. 4(i) presents the humidity sensitivity at different valleys for different sensors. It can be clearly observed that the LD-HCF humidity sensor filled with PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber exhibits higher humidity sensitivity under UV excitation. Its humidity sensitivity is improved by about ∼94-folds compared with that of LD-HCF structure and ∼2.3-folds compared with that of the sensor without UV excitation. On the other hand, Fig. 4(j) shows the spectrum drift of the LD-HCF humidity sensor filled with PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber in 40 %RH humidity environment over 60 minutes. Experimental results show that the sensor does not show the obvious characteristic peak drift during a long time measuring process and performs the excellent long-term stability and reliability.

During the experiment, the humidity in the closed air chamber was kept at 30 %RH. Fig. 5(a) shows the trend of the sensor spectrum changing with temperature under UV irradiation. As the temperature increases, the spectrum of the sensor shifts blue. Fig. 5(b) shows the temperature sensitivity curve of the sensor. The sensitivity of the sensor to temperature is only 1.19 pm/℃. This indicates that the humidity detection error caused by the temperature change of 1 ℃ is only 0.007 %RH, so the influence of temperature on humidity detection can be basically ignored. The reason for the low temperature sensitivity of the sensor may be that a large number of nanointerfaces between CQDs and PVA matrix provide effective phonon scattering and heat transfer paths [42,43], which enable the material to quickly reach thermal equilibrium when the temperature changes, maintain the stability of physical and chemical properties and reduce the thermal expansion effect, thus reducing the temperature response ability of the sensor. The consistency of the sensor is very important in its practical application. In order to study the consistency of the sensor, sensor-2 and sensor-3 were prepared. Fig. 5(c) and (d) show the spectral responses of sensor-2 and sensor-3 under different humidity levels, respectively. It can be seen that their spectra shift blue with increasing humidity. Fig. 5(e) presents the humidity sensitivity curves of the three sensors prepared in this study. The humidity sensitivities of these sensors are 0.172 nm/%RH, 0.171 nm/%RH, and 0.174 nm/%RH, respectively, indicating that the sensitivities are essentially the same. Therefore, the sensor demonstrates good consistency.

Fig. 2(g) shows the respiratory monitoring system. Respiratory monitoring system was presented in SI-2 in the supplementary information. As shown in Fig. 2(a) -(c), the sensor is fixed inside the mask by the clamp after the heat shrink tube package to avoid distortion and stress, and to ensure that the sensor will not be displaced and deformed during use. In addition, the acrylic protective layer on the outer layer of the fiber has excellent adhesion and flexibility, allowing it to improve the axial tensile strength of the fiber without affecting the light transmission, providing additional mechanical protection. In addition, since stress and torsion are considered DC signals while respiratory signals are AC signals, the difference between these signals allows for the decoupling of interference signals from effective signals [44], ensuring the accuracy of respiratory monitoring.

The humidity characteristic curve of the respiratory monitoring mask is shown in Fig. 4(k). It is verified that the packaged sensor has the high sensitivity to humidity and excellent recovery performance by precisely controlling the humidity in the air chamber. Fig. 4(l) displays the sensitivity curve of the respiratory monitoring mask, indicating that it has the excellent recoverability. The sensitivity is 0.0039 V/%RH with increasing humidity and 0.0038 V/%RH with decreasing humidity, which is very close to each other. The time response curve of the respiratory monitoring mask based on PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber is shown in the Fig. 6(a). The mask has a response time of 0.232 s and a recovery time of 0.252 s.

Human respiratory patterns are diverse. This study captures and analyzes the respiratory signals in different states, including walking (Fig. 6(b)), resting (Fig. 6(c)), coughing (Fig. 6(d)), running (Fig. 6(e)), apnea 1 time (Fig. 6(f)) and apnea 3 times (Fig. 6(g)). Frequency distribution information of the signals was obtained by performing FFT analysis on these signals in the time domain. The experimental results of respiratory monitoring are as follows: the respiratory frequency of individuals during walking is 0.765 Hz, resting 0.329 Hz, coughing 0.111 Hz, and running 1.519 Hz. In addition, the sensor can detect the pause process during breathing. Fig. 6(f) is the output curve of the sensor when apnea occurs once, and the time of apnea is recorded as 21.09 s. Fig. 6(g) shows the output curve of apnea for three times, and the pause time is 18.75 s, 20.3 s and 23.44 s respectively. The experimental results reveal the significant differences in human respiratory frequency under different activity states. In addition, the volunteers experienced three continuous motion states: sitting, walking and running, as shown in Fig. 6(h), and their corresponding frequencies were 0.1638 Hz, 0.4671 Hz and 1.5348 Hz respectively (Fig. 6(i)). By analyzing the frequency component of the respiratory signal, the information of different breathing states can be obtained.

The recognition of respiratory patterns is very important for monitoring the physiological and psychological state of individuals. Neural network can classify the respiratory patterns with high precision. This study uses Whale Optimization Algorithm (WOA), one-dimensional CNN(1D CNN) and LSTM to find the best combination of learning rate, number of hidden layer nodes and regularization coefficient to optimize the neural network model. The frequency, amplitude, rise time and pulse width distribution of 150 groups of respiratory signals are studied, as shown in the Fig. S2. There are strong cross-interference in the data of these features, which may reduce the accuracy of respiratory recognition depending on the traditional feature extraction and classification algorithms.

The main function of 1D CNN is to extract local features or patterns from input sequences and reduce the dimension of input data, thus reducing the computational complexity of LSTM network. WOA is an optimization algorithm based on the foraging behavior of whales. It optimizes the parameters by simulating the process of whales searching for food, aiming to further improve the performance of the 1D CNN-LSTM network. This algorithm helps find the best solutions in complex parameter search spaces. Combination of WOA and 1D CNN-LSTM can better overcome the training difficulties, overfitting, and other challenges. The principle of WOA and WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural network structure was presented in SI-3 and SI-4 in the supplementary information, respectively. Fig. 7 show the architecture of respiratory data classification model based on WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural network.

The study collected 150 sets of human respiratory data. Each breathing pattern consists of 25 pieces of data. The training set capacity of the network is 70 %. The test set has a capacity of 30 % and is used to evaluate the accuracy of the network to new data, thus evaluating the generalization ability and classification performance of the network.

Walking, running, resting, coughing, apnea 1 time and apnea 3 times are labeled as 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 respectively. Fig. 8(a) illustrates the classification results distribution of the 1D CNN-LSTM network on the training set. The 1D CNN-LSTM network achieved 89.81 % accuracy on the training set. Fig. 8(b) shows that the classification accuracy of 1D CNN-LSTM network on the test set is 85.71 %. The results for the WOA-1D CNN-LSTM network on the training set are displayed in Fig. 8(c). It achieved 100 % accuracy on the training set. As shown in Fig. 8(d), the performance of the WOA-1D CNN-LSTM network improved further for the test set, achieving a classification accuracy of 97.62 %, significantly outperforming the 1D CNN-LSTM network. This is mainly attributed to the outstanding global search capability of the WOA optimization algorithm, which helps to better find the combination of global optimal learning rates, hidden layer nodes, and regularization coefficients.

Fig. 8(e) compares the accuracy convergence curves and loss function curves between the WOA-1D CNN-LSTM network and the 1D CNN-LSTM network. The results indicate that the WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural network achieves the preset classification accuracy in only 136 iterations, while the 1D CNN-LSTM network requires 415 iterations. It significantly improved the convergence speed of the network and reduced the required number of iterations after optimizing the network using the WOA algorithm. The confusion matrixs for the test sets of the 1D CNN-LSTM and WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural network is shown in Fig. 8(f) and (g). The results demonstrate that the WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural network performs better on the test set, achieving the higher accuracy and needing fewer misclassified samples.

Table 1 compares the optical fiber humidity sensor proposed in this study with the previously reported humidity sensor. Compared with the humidity sensor with aingle-nanowire sensor array, the sensor proposed in this study has faster response time and recovery time [45]. Polyelectrolyte-based humidity sensor has a fast response time, but its limit of detection (LOD) is 33 %RH. In contrast, the sensor mentioned in this study has a lower LOD [46]. The humidity sensor based on polymer composite nanofibers and the microwave humidity sensor based on carbon dots-decorated MOF-derived porous CoO require a longer recovery time compared to the sensor mentioned in this study [47,48]. Although the humidity sensor based on flexible fiber optics has a fast response time, its sensitivity is low for humidity detection below 40 %RH, which limits its application range [49]. A paper-based capacitive humidity sensor has high humidity sensitivity, but it is insensitive to low humidity and requires longer response and recovery times [50]. Although the optical fiber humidity sensor coated with a poly allylamine hydrochloride (PAH)/SiO2 layer has a relatively wide humidity detection range, its performance in response time and recovery time is still not advantageous compared with this study [51]. Photonic crystal fiber (PCF) based on graphene oxide (GO) coating has high humidity sensitivity, but it also has the problem of long time response [52]. Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensor coated with polyimide layer can detect high concentration humidity, but its sensitivity is relatively low [53]. In addition, the linearity of the sensitivity curve of these sensors is poor, while the linearity of the sensitivity curve of the sensor in this study is 0.996. This indicates that the optical fiber humidity sensor based on nano-carbon quantum water gel microfiber still shows good detection ability in low humidity environments. Furthermore, the breath recognition scheme based on WOA-1D CNN-LSTM neural network in this study can achieve a recognition rate of 97.62 %, which is not reported in other studies. The humidity sensor discussed in this study demonstrates fast response time, excellent linearity, and capability for breath pattern recognition. Compared with the above-mentioned sensors, it may be more suitable for practical applications.

In this study, a hydrogel microfiber composed of CQDs and PVA was successfully prepared and injected into the hollow core structure of LD-HCF to create PVA-CQDs hydrogel microfiber. The sensitivity of 0.1721 nm/%RH is exhibited within a humidity range of 10 %RH to 80 %RH and demonstrated with an excellent stability. A respiratory monitoring mask was developed based on this sensor, featuring a response time of 0.232 s and a recovery time of 0.252 s. The mask was used for real-time monitoring of human respiration, successfully identifying the respiration patterns during activities, such as walking, resting, coughing, running, apnea 1 time and apnea 3 times. The recognition of respiration patterns was achieved using the WOA-1D CNN-LSTM model, resulting in a recognition rate of 97.62 %. This respiratory monitoring device offers the advantages of low cost, resistance to electromagnetic interference, rapid response, and high recognition accuracy, making it highly promising for applications in the field of medical monitoring equipment.
Yannan Wang: Writing – original draft. Jin Li: Writing – review & editing. Liyun Qin: Conceptualization. Zhenyu Yuan: Visualization. Fanli Meng: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62071112, 62033002), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities in China (N2201008), the 111 Project (B16009), and the Hebei Natural Science Foundation (F2020501040).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136644.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced thermoelectric performance of single-walled carbon nanotubes/polyaniline hybrid nanocomposites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yao, N.K., Chen, L., Zhang, W., Liufu, S., Chen, X., 2010. Enhanced thermoelectric performance of single-walled carbon nanotubes/polyaniline hybrid nanocomposites. ACS Nano. 4(4), 2445-2451.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Closely packed stretchable ultrasound array fabricated with surface charge engineering for contactless gesture and materials detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dutta, A., Niu, Z., Abdullah, A.M., Tiwari, N., Biswas, M.A., Li, B., Lorestani, F., Jing, Y., Cheng, H.K., 2024. Closely packed stretchable ultrasound array fabricated with surface charge engineering for contactless gesture and materials detection. Adv. Sci. 11(15), 2303403.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A disposable breath sensing tube with on-tube single-nanowire sensor array for on-site detection of exhaled breath biomarkers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wu, C., Wang, W., Hong, C., Hwang, K.C., 2016. A disposable breath sensing tube with on-tube single-nanowire sensor array for on-site detection of exhaled breath biomarkers. Lab Chip. 16(22): 4395-4405.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrafast response polyelectrolyte humidity sensor for respiration monitoring

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dai, J., Zhao, H., Lin, X., Liu, S., Liu, Y., Liu, X., Fei, T., Zhang, T., 2019. Ultrafast response polyelectrolyte humidity sensor for respiration monitoring. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 11(6): 6483-6490.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High sensitive and fast response humidity sensor based on polymer composite nanofibers for breath monitoring and non-contact sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, X., Zhuang, Z., Qi, D., Zhao, C., 2020. High sensitive and fast response humidity sensor based on polymer composite nanofibers for breath monitoring and non-contact sensing. Sens. Actuators, B. 330: 129239.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave humidity sensor based on carbon dots-decorated MOF-derived porous Co3O4 for breath monitoring and finger moisture detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yu, H., Wang, C., Meng, F., Xiao, J., Liang, J., Kim, H., Bae, S., Zou, D., Kim, E.S., Kim, N., Zhao, M., Li, B., 2021. Microwave humidity sensor based on carbon dots-decorated MOF-derived porous Co3O4 for breath monitoring and finger moisture detection. Carbon, 183: 578-589.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Wearable breath monitoring based on a flexible fiber-optic humidity sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bao, W., Chen, F., Lai, H., Liu, S., Wang, Y., 2021. Wearable breath monitoring based on a flexible fiber-optic humidity sensor. Sens. Actuators, B. 349: 130794.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-sensitivity paper-based capacitive humidity sensors for respiratory monitoring

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Song, Z., Li,S., Hou, B., Cheng,Z., Xue, Y., Chen, B., 2023. High-sensitivity paper-based capacitive humidity sensors for respiratory monitoring. IEEE Sens. J. 23(3): 2291-2302.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical fibre sensor for simultaneous temperature and relative humidity measurement: towards absolute humidity evaluation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: He, C., Korposh, S., Correia, R., Liu, L., Hayes-Gill, B.R., Morgan, S.P., 2021. Optical fibre sensor for simultaneous temperature and relative humidity measurement: Towards absolute humidity evaluation. Sens. Actuators, B, 344: 130154.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fiber temperature and humidity sensor based on photonic crystal fiber coated with graphene oxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, J., Tong, Z., Jing, L., Zhang, W., Qin, J., Liu, J., 2020. Fiber temperature and humidity sensor based on photonic crystal fiber coated with graphene oxide. Opt. Commun., 467: 125707.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A humidity fiber sensor based on both end-sides of a fiber Bragg grating coated with polyimide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xie, Z., Yan, H., Li, Y., Zhao, X., 2020. A humidity fiber sensor based on both end-sides of a fiber Bragg grating coated with polyimide. Opt. Fiber Technol., 57: 102220.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yannan Wang was born in Zhangjiakou, Hebei Province, China, in 1998. He received the Master’s degree in Detection Technology and Automation Device in July, 2022. He is currently purchasing a Doctor’s Degree in Detection Technology and Automatic Devices supervised by A/Prof. Jin Li in the College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang, China. His research interests are microfiber sensor, optical integration, Optoelectronics detection and automatic control methods.

Jin Li was born in Yuncheng, Shanxi Province, China, in 1983. He has received the Bachelor’s degree in Electronics Science and Technology in 2007, the Master’s degree and Doctor’s degree in Physical Electronics respectively in 2009 and 2014, at Harbin Institute of Technology (HIT), China.He servers the Research Fellow and Ph.D. supervisor in the College of Mechanical and Electronic Engineering, Northwest A&F University, China. His research interests include micro/nano-structured photonics, nanomaterials’ nonlinear optics, micro/nanofiber sensors and integrated optical devices.Dr. Li has charged for more than 10 national and province projects and published more than 50 SCI-indexed papers in the role of first or corresponding author.

Fanli Meng was born in Tieling, Liaoning Province China, in 1980. He received his Bachelor’s degree from Nanjing University of Science and Technology in 2002; In 2005, he obtained a Master's degree in Hefei Institute of Intelligent Machinery, Chinese Academy of Sciences; In 2009, he obtained a doctor's degree at the University of Science and Technology of China.He worked as a research Fellow in Hefei Institute of Intelligent Machinery, Chinese Academy of Sciences from 2005 to 2017. Since December 2016, he has been a professor in the College of Information Science and Engineering at Northeastern University, China.Prof. Meng has been in charge of 4 projects supported by National Natural Science foundation of China. He has published more than 90 papers in the SCI indexed peer review journals, such as Sensors and Actuators B, IEEE Transactions on Nanotechnology, TrAC-Trends in Analytical Chemistry, Analytica Chimica Acta and IEEE Sensors Journal. Up to now, all these papers have been cited by others for more than 3000 times and the h-index is 32. Among them, three were selected to be ESI highly cited papers. The most highly cited paper was cited over 400 times by others. He has got 8 authorized patents. He also achieved many awards such as the CAIA Youth Award from China Association for Instrumental Analysis, the first grade of prize in Anhui Natural Science Award, the Anhui Outstanding Academic Paper Award in Natural Science, etc. He is an associated editor of the journal of Micro & Nano Letters and is also a member of the editorial board of the Journal of Sensors, Acta Automatica Sinica, etc. Main research areas include: gas sensing nanomaterials and devices, chemical sensors and trace detection technology.
